skill.name: tdd-workflows-tdd-green
skill.description: Implement the minimal code needed to make failing tests pass in the TDD green phase.
skill.summary: Guides developers to implement minimal code to make tests pass during TDD green phase
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 105 detected patterns are false positives from code examples in educational documentation. The skill contains only instructional markdown content with code samples demonstrating TDD patterns. No executable code, scripts, or runtime behavior exists. The 'MD5', 'SHA1', 'SQLite', 'backticks', and 'fetch' patterns appear strictly within code blocks as teaching examples for proper TDD implementation across multiple programming languages.

content.value_statement: Learn to implement minimal code that makes failing tests pass during TDD green phase. Avoid over-engineering and keep implementations simple.
actual_capabilities: Guides implementation of minimal code to make failing tests pass
Teaches Fake It, Obvious Implementation, and Triangulation techniques
Provides language-specific patterns for JavaScript, Python, Java, C#, Go, and Ruby
Includes decision frameworks for choosing between fake and real implementations
Offers complete examples from minimal to production-ready code
Contains anti-patterns to avoid during green phase implementation
limitations: Documentation only - does not execute code or run tests automatically
Requires developer to manually implement suggested patterns
Focused solely on green phase - does not cover test creation or refactoring
Examples are instructional and may need adaptation for specific project contexts

use_cases.title: Learning TDD Fundamentals
use_cases.description: New developers learning test-driven development can use this skill to understand the green phase methodology and avoid common mistakes like over-engineering

use_cases.title: Speeding Up Feature Implementation
use_cases.description: Experienced developers can quickly implement minimal passing tests without getting bogged down in premature optimization during initial development

use_cases.title: Team TDD Training
use_cases.description: Development teams adopting TDD can use the structured patterns and examples to establish consistent green phase practices across the organization

prompt_templates.title: Basic Green Phase Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me implement the minimal code needed to make these failing tests pass. Focus on simplicity and avoid over-engineering.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have failing tests and need the simplest implementation to make them pass

prompt_templates.title: Language-Specific Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to implement minimal code for these tests using [JavaScript/Python/Java/C#/Go/Ruby]. Follow TDD green phase best practices.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need language-specific patterns for your tech stack

prompt_templates.title: Fake vs Real Implementation Decision
prompt_templates.prompt: Should I use a fake implementation or real implementation for these tests? Explain the trade-offs and recommend the best approach.
prompt_templates.scenario: You are unsure whether to fake it or implement real functionality

prompt_templates.title: Review and Simplify Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my implementation and tell me if it's truly minimal for the green phase or if I'm over-engineering. Suggest simplifications if needed.
prompt_templates.scenario: You want to verify your implementation follows green phase principles

output_examples.input: I have a failing test for user creation. Help me implement minimal code.
output: Here's the minimal implementation using Fake It pattern:
This passes the test with a hard-coded ID. You can evolve to real ID generation when more tests require it.

output_examples.input: Should I add error handling to this green phase implementation?
output: No - in green phase, only add what tests require. If tests don't check error scenarios, skip error handling for now.
Add it during refactor phase when you have tests that validate error paths.

best_practices: Implement the absolute minimum code that makes tests pass - avoid adding features or optimizations not required by tests
Run tests after each small change to verify progress and catch issues early
Document technical debt and shortcuts taken during green phase for later refactoring
anti_patterns: Gold plating - adding unrequested features or enhancements during green phase
Premature refactoring - changing code structure while tests are still failing
Implementing design patterns or architectural decisions before tests require them

faq.question: What is the TDD green phase?
faq.answer: The green phase is the second step in test-driven development where you write the minimal code needed to make failing tests pass. The focus is on simplicity and speed, not perfection.

faq.question: Should I fake it or implement real code?
faq.answer: Start with fake implementations (hard-coded values) for the first test. Move to real code when subsequent tests reveal a pattern that requires generalization. This keeps you focused on passing tests rather than over-thinking design.

faq.question: Can I refactor during the green phase?
faq.answer: Avoid refactoring while tests are failing. First make all tests pass with minimal code, then refactor in the separate refactor phase. This keeps the two concerns distinct and manageable.

faq.question: What if my implementation feels too simple?
faq.answer: That is exactly the point of green phase. Embrace simplicity. Trust that the refactor phase will improve the design. Focus on making tests pass with the least code possible.

faq.question: Do I need to add error handling in green phase?
faq.answer: Only add error handling if tests explicitly require it. If tests do not cover error scenarios, skip error handling for now. Add it during refactor phase when you have comprehensive test coverage.

faq.question: How do I know when green phase is complete?
faq.answer: Green phase is complete when all tests pass. At that point, stop implementing and move to refactor phase. Do not add extra features or improvements beyond what tests require.
